nativeRange.habitat: Shallow surge zones, gutters and exposed seaward reef flats; typically reported at depths of roughly 0–15 m, most commonly 1–3 m

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer on filamentous and turf algae in the wild; in aquaria, dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods are generally advised at least three times per week to support immunity and reduce aggression. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis, krill, and brine shrimp once established, but newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods initially and benefit from heavy algae availability on live rock to stimulate grazing.

goodWith: established tankmates introduced before the tang
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured fish
small timid fish in undersized systems

summary: A boldly striped Indo-Pacific surgeonfish prized for its yellow-and-blue patterning but widely considered one of the most challenging tangs in the hobby. Generally advised only for experienced keepers with very large, mature systems; have been known to be highly aggressive toward other tangs and similarly shaped fish.
careNotes:
The Clown Tang (Acanthurus lineatus) is a large, fast-swimming surgeonfish from shallow Indo-Pacific reef flats and surge zones. Adults are generally reported at around 30–38 cm in well-kept aquariums, with FishBase listing a wild maximum of 38 cm TL. Because the species is an active, territorial swimmer, a system of at least roughly 1000 L (around 250+ US gallons) with a long footprint is commonly advised; sources widely repeat 250 US gal as a starting point and specialist references such as ReefApp recommend 1000 L outright.

Water quality is the single most important variable for long-term success. Stable, well-oxygenated reef conditions (around 23–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025, dKH 8–12) are generally advised, with strong flow to mimic the surge zones the species occupies in the wild. The Clown Tang is widely reported as prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) and to ich, particularly when diet is protein-heavy or water quality slips.

Diet should lean strongly toward marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) on a clip, spirulina-based prepared foods, and macroalgae on live rock are generally considered essential; meaty foods such as mysis and krill can be offered as supplements rather than staples. Newly imported specimens frequently refuse prepared foods on arrival — feeding difficulty is among the most commonly cited reasons for losses in the first weeks, and an established tank with grazable algae greatly improves acclimation.

The species is well known for high conspecific and inter-tang aggression. It is generally advised to keep only one Clown Tang per system and to avoid mixing with other Acanthurus surgeonfishes. Introducing the Clown Tang last, after smaller and more timid tankmates are established, is the commonly suggested compatibility strategy.

Like all acanthurids, this species has a sharp caudal-peduncle spine (the 'scalpel') used in defence; FishBase additionally classifies the caudal spine as venomous and notes a risk of painful wounds to handlers. Handle with care during transfers and net carefully to avoid the spine catching.

Lifespan in the wild is exceptional (FishBase reports up to 46 years), but captive longevity is commonly reported under 10 years, often substantially less when husbandry is imperfect. Reproduction in captivity is not documented in the hobby literature.

Source cross-check notes: Adult size is consistent across sources at roughly 38 cm max (FishBase, LiveAquaria, ReefApp, fishlaboratory) with saltwateraquariumblog citing 30.5–35.6 cm; range averaged to 30–38 cm. Tank minimum varied from 250 US gal (≈946 L) at most retail sources to 1000 L at ReefApp; biased toward the larger value per the active-swimmer / high-aggression context — 1000 L selected (nearest allowed nice-value at or above the average). Temperature ranged from 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) at LiveAquaria/fishlaboratory to 24–30°C at FishBase; averaged to 23–27°C. Care level ranged from 'Difficult' (LiveAquaria) to 'Expert' (fishlaboratory, saltwateraquariumblog); set to 'expert' given the consistently reported acclimation losses and HLLE susceptibility.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Clown Tang is not documented in available aquarium-hobby literature. In the wild, the species forms spawning aggregations but pair-spawns, with courtship reported at various times and a morning spawning peak (FishBase). Maturity is reached at around 18 cm. Reproduction in home aquaria is considered effectively impossible given the tank volumes, conditioning, and larval rearing required.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Clown Tang. Provided adult size (15 in), tank minimum (250 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Difficult), diet (herbivore), temperament (semi-aggressive toward similar fish), and reef-safe status.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (38 cm TL max, 25 cm common), native range (Indo-Pacific from East Africa to Hawaiian Islands), depth (0–15 m), temperature (24–30°C), habitat (surge zones of exposed seaward reefs), reproductive biology (paired spawning within aggregations), wild longevity (up to 46 years), maturity (18 cm), IUCN Least Concern, and explicit note of a venomous caudal spine capable of painful wounds.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (38 cm), minimum aquarium volume (1000 L), reef-safe status, aggressive temperament toward similar species, and herbivorous diet with algae as primary and crustaceans as supplement. Tank volume of 1000 L is notably higher than retail sources and was used as the upper bound for the cautious tank-size pick.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Confirmed tank minimum (250 gal / 946 L), adult size (12–14 in), aggressive temperament, reef-safe status, captive lifespan under 10 years, expert care level, HLLE and ich susceptibility, jumping/escape behaviour, and the well-documented feeding-strike pattern in newly imported specimens.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Confirmed tank minimum (250 gal, longer rather than deep), adult size (up to 15 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), advanced/expert care level, herbivorous diet with seaweed and meaty supplements, territorial behaviour increasing with maturity, reef-safe status, HLLE susceptibility, and lifespan of 25–30 years wild vs under 10 years captive.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available aquarium-hobby literature. Wild reproduction is by paired spawning within larger aggregations (FishBase). Tank volumes, conditioning, and pelagic larval rearing required make home-aquarium reproduction effectively unattainable.
